purpose: Coach-only seed strategy bank for ReflectLoop Coach P0.

strategies.title: Revoice, Pause, Probe
strategies.summary: Teacher revoices a student contribution, waits, then probes for reasoning before moving on.
strategies.teacher_move: Say, 'So you are saying ___. What makes you think that?' then wait silently for 3-5 seconds.
strategies.student_move: Student clarifies reasoning or adds evidence.
coach_use_when: Teacher acknowledges answers but moves on quickly.
Student responses are short or unsupported.
Observation notes show limited uptake.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher takes up a student idea and uses it as the basis for the next question.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher allows 3-5 seconds after the probe before speaking again.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks whether the student can explain the reasoning behind the answer.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students revisit the same idea through explanation rather than a second task.

implementation_steps: Select one key question in the lesson.
After the first student answer, revoice it neutrally.
Ask one reasoning probe.
Wait 3-5 seconds before inviting more detail.
cautions: Do not revoice as correction.
Keep tone curious rather than evaluative.

strategies.title: Redirect for Peer Build
strategies.summary: Teacher redirects a student idea to peers so the class builds on it collectively.
strategies.teacher_move: Ask, 'Who can build on or question that idea?' instead of confirming the answer immediately.
strategies.student_move: Students respond to each other's ideas, not only to the teacher.
coach_use_when: Discussion is teacher-student-teacher-student.
Few student-to-student links are observed.
Teacher wants more collective reasoning.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher redirects a student response to another student and prompts connection or challenge.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher waits after redirecting instead of filling the silence.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher listens for whether multiple students can extend, question, or restate the idea.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students encounter the concept again through peer explanation and comparison.

implementation_steps: Identify one discussion moment where students usually answer only the teacher.
After a student response, redirect it to the group.
Invite one peer to build and one peer to question or connect.
Summarize the class-built idea.
cautions: Avoid forcing disagreement; allow building, questioning, or connecting.
Protect psychological safety by normalizing partial ideas.

strategies.title: Hinge Question Re-Exposure
strategies.summary: Teacher uses a planned hinge question to decide whether students need another exposure to the concept.
strategies.teacher_move: Ask one diagnostic question that reveals the misconception, then choose a quick re-exposure task based on responses.
strategies.student_move: Students answer individually and then revisit the idea through a second representation or example.
coach_use_when: Teacher repeats explanations without checking understanding.
Misconceptions appear late in independent work.
Lesson pacing overrides evidence of understanding.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher uses student responses to decide the next instructional move.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives students enough time to answer individually before discussion.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher uses a hinge question to gather evidence before moving forward.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students receive a second targeted exposure when evidence shows partial understanding.

implementation_steps: Write one hinge question before the lesson.
Decide what response pattern means 'move on' versus 're-expose'.
Ask students to answer individually.
Use responses to select a brief second example, model, or peer explanation.
cautions: The hinge question should test understanding, not recall only.
Use wrong answers as instructional information, not failure evidence.

strategies.title: Wait Time with Everyone Rehearses
strategies.summary: Teacher builds deliberate wait time by asking all students to rehearse an answer before discussion.
strategies.teacher_move: Say, 'Take 20 seconds to think or jot. I will ask several people to share how they are thinking.'
strategies.student_move: All students prepare a response before selected students speak.
coach_use_when: Same few students answer most questions.
Teacher wait time is under three seconds.
Teacher wants broader participation without cold-call pressure.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher invites multiple prepared responses and probes differences in thinking.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher provides explicit private thinking time before public response.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher samples several prepared responses to gauge class understanding.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students encounter the question first privately, then publicly through peer responses.

implementation_steps: Choose one high-value question.
Give silent think or jot time.
Invite several responses.
Probe one response for reasoning and one for a different approach.
cautions: Do not shorten the wait time when silence feels uncomfortable.
Avoid praising only speed or confidence.

strategies.title: Connect Prior, Current, Future Learning
strategies.summary: Teacher prompts students to connect today's idea to a prior exposure and a future application.
strategies.teacher_move: Ask, 'Where have we seen this before, and where might we use it next?'
strategies.student_move: Students name links across lessons, examples, or contexts.
coach_use_when: Lessons feel isolated.
Students complete tasks but do not transfer ideas.
Teacher wants to strengthen cumulative talk.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher takes up student connections and uses them to deepen the current concept.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher allows thinking time for students to retrieve prior learning.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks whether students can connect the concept across contexts.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students revisit the concept across spaced lessons and varied contexts.

implementation_steps: Identify the prior exposure students should remember.
Ask students to connect prior and current learning.
Invite one future-use prediction.
Record or summarize the strongest connection.
cautions: If students cannot connect, provide a cue rather than answering for them.
Keep the focus on meaning, not just lesson chronology.

strategies.title: Hedged Challenge Alternative
strategies.summary: Teacher introduces a respectful alternative view to help students test and refine reasoning.
strategies.teacher_move: Say, 'One possible challenge to that is ___. How might we respond?'
strategies.student_move: Students evaluate, defend, revise, or qualify an idea.
coach_use_when: Discussion remains agreeable but shallow.
Teacher avoids challenging student assumptions.
Students need practice revising ideas.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher challenges the idea, not the student, and invites response or revision.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher pauses after posing the alternative so students can think before defending or revising.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks whether students understand the limits or conditions of an idea.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students re-encounter the idea through contrast, exception, or alternative case.

implementation_steps: Choose one student idea worth deepening.
Frame a challenge as a possible alternative, not a correction.
Ask students how the idea might need to change.
Summarize the refined understanding.
cautions: Challenge assumptions without embarrassing students.
Use hedging language to preserve safety and openness.

strategies.title: Student-Generated Re-Exposure Examples
strategies.summary: Students create new examples or representations to show transfer after initial instruction.
strategies.teacher_move: Ask students to create a new example, diagram, or scenario that shows the same concept in a different context.
strategies.student_move: Students apply the concept beyond the original teacher example.
coach_use_when: Students can follow worked examples but struggle to transfer.
Teacher wants evidence beyond correct completion.
Multiple exposures are present but mostly teacher-led.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher uses student-generated examples to probe transfer and connect ideas.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives sufficient creation time before discussion.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks whether students can generate a valid new example or representation.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students independently create varied exposures and transfer the concept across contexts.

implementation_steps: After modeling, ask students to create a new example or representation.
Have pairs compare whether each example fits the concept.
Invite one pair to explain a strong example and one borderline example.
Probe the criteria that make the example valid.
cautions: Provide criteria so examples are not arbitrary.
Use borderline examples for reasoning, not correction.

strategies.title: Academic Recast and Rehearsal
strategies.summary: Teacher recasts a student's everyday language into academic English, then asks the student or pair to rehearse the improved sentence.
strategies.teacher_move: Say, 'You said ___. In the language of today's lesson, we could say ___. Practise saying that with your partner.'
strategies.student_move: Students repeat, adapt, and use the academic phrasing in a partner response.
coach_use_when: EAL students have the idea but lack the academic English to express it.
Teacher accepts one-word or informal responses without language development.
Lesson talk does not build subject-specific vocabulary.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher takes up student meaning and upgrades the language without dismissing the original contribution.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives students rehearsal time before expecting public academic speech.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher listens for whether students can reuse the academic phrase accurately.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students hear, rehearse, and reuse the same academic phrase in one lesson.

implementation_steps: Choose one target sentence frame linked to the lesson objective.
Listen for a student idea expressed in everyday language.
Recast the idea in academic English while preserving the student's meaning.
Ask pairs to rehearse the recast sentence and use it in a response.
observable_indicators: Teacher explicitly names the upgraded phrase.
Students orally rehearse the phrase before whole-class sharing.
At least one later response uses the phrase.
cautions: Do not imply the student's home or everyday language is wrong.
Keep the recast short enough for students to use immediately.

strategies.title: Build and Challenge Sentence Stems
strategies.summary: Teacher posts and practises two talk stems so students can build on or respectfully challenge ideas.
strategies.teacher_move: Model: 'I want to build on ___ because ___' and 'I see it differently because ___'; require pairs to use one stem during discussion.
strategies.student_move: Students use a stem to connect, extend, or question a peer's idea.
coach_use_when: Students answer the teacher but do not speak to each other.
Discussion lacks cumulative or reciprocal talk.
EAL students need language frames for extended talk.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher redirects responses to peers and prompts use of a build or challenge stem.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives pairs time to prepare a stem-based response.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher samples peer responses to check whether students can explain or challenge the idea.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students repeatedly use the same oracy stems across tasks and lessons.

implementation_steps: Select two stems only: one for building and one for challenge.
Model each stem with a subject-specific example.
Ask pairs to prepare one build or challenge response.
Invite two pairs to share and briefly name the talk move used.
observable_indicators: Stems are visible.
Students speak to or about each other's ideas.
Teacher names the talk move rather than only judging correctness.
cautions: Do not introduce too many stems at once.
Treat challenge as testing ideas, not criticising classmates.

strategies.title: Think-Pair-Public Share
strategies.summary: Teacher protects private thinking and pair rehearsal before asking students to speak publicly in English.
strategies.teacher_move: Say, 'Think silently for 20 seconds, practise your answer with your partner, then I will ask two pairs to share.'
strategies.student_move: Students prepare language privately, rehearse with a partner, and then share a clearer response.
coach_use_when: EAL students are reluctant to answer publicly.
Teacher moves from textbook question directly to cold public response.
Only confident students participate.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher uses rehearsed pair responses as the basis for probing and connecting ideas.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives both silent think time and partner rehearsal time.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher samples rehearsed responses from multiple pairs.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students encounter the question silently, orally with a peer, then publicly.

implementation_steps: Choose one textbook question worth discussing.
Add silent think time.
Add partner rehearsal using a target phrase.
Ask two pairs to share and probe one reasoning detail.
observable_indicators: Teacher does not take the first hand immediately.
Most students rehearse orally before public sharing.
Public answers are longer than one word.
cautions: Keep the sequence brisk so it supports rather than replaces lesson flow.
Circulate during pair rehearsal to listen for vocabulary barriers.

strategies.title: Three-Word Vocabulary Pre-Teach
strategies.summary: Teacher selects three high-leverage words before the textbook task and teaches meaning, pronunciation, and use.
strategies.teacher_move: Before the task, teach three words using 'say it, explain it, show it, use it in today's sentence.'
strategies.student_move: Students pronounce each word, explain it in simple language, and use it in a sentence linked to the task.
coach_use_when: Textbook language blocks access to the task.
Teacher assumes students know key words because they appear in the book.
EAL students copy answers without understanding vocabulary.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher prompts students to use the target words in responses later in the lesson.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher allows rehearsal time before asking students to use new words.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks pronunciation, meaning, and sentence use before the task begins.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students meet each word through pronunciation, explanation, visual cue, and sentence use.

implementation_steps: Scan the textbook page and choose only three words essential for understanding.
Prepare a student-friendly explanation and quick visual or gesture for each.
Students say each word and use it in a short sentence.
Return to the words during the task and require students to use at least one.
observable_indicators: Three words are visible.
Students say the words aloud.
Teacher checks use, not just copying of definitions.
cautions: Do not overload students with a long vocabulary list.
Prioritise words that unlock the task, not every unfamiliar word.

strategies.title: Fast Frayer with Non-Example
strategies.summary: Teacher uses a four-part word routine: meaning, example, non-example, and sentence.
strategies.teacher_move: For one key word, ask: 'What does it mean? What is an example? What is not an example? Use it in today's sentence.'
strategies.student_move: Students distinguish the word from a close non-example and apply it in context.
coach_use_when: Students can repeat definitions but misuse words.
Vocabulary teaching is copying-only.
Teacher needs a short routine for deeper word understanding.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher probes why an example fits and why a non-example does not.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives students time to compare example and non-example.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks whether students can classify and use the word, not only define it.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students revisit the word through definition, example, contrast, and application.

implementation_steps: Choose one word central to the lesson.
Give a student-friendly meaning.
Ask students to identify or create an example and non-example.
Require a sentence using the word in the lesson context.
observable_indicators: Students explain why something is or is not an example.
The word is used in a subject sentence.
Teacher probes misconceptions about the word.
cautions: Use one word at a time when students are overloaded.
Choose non-examples that reveal meaning, not trick cases.

strategies.title: Word-Part Meaning Routine
strategies.summary: Teacher teaches a prefix, suffix, or root so students can infer related vocabulary across the textbook.
strategies.teacher_move: Say, 'This part of the word means ___. Where else do we see it, and what might the full word mean?'
strategies.student_move: Students break a word into parts and infer meaning before checking in context.
coach_use_when: Students meet many related academic words.
Teacher translates or defines each word separately.
EAL learners need tools for unfamiliar textbook vocabulary.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher uses student guesses to connect word parts to meaning.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives students time to inspect the word before giving the answer.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher asks students to apply the word part to a second word.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students reuse the word-part strategy across multiple related words.

implementation_steps: Select one useful prefix, suffix, or root from the textbook page.
Explain its meaning briefly.
Apply it to the target word.
Ask students to find or predict another related word.
observable_indicators: Students mark or underline word parts.
Students infer before being told.
Teacher returns to the word part later.
cautions: Avoid obscure etymology; keep the word part useful and repeated.
Check meaning in sentence context after inference.

strategies.title: Two-Minute Vocabulary Retrieval
strategies.summary: Teacher revisits previous vocabulary at the start or middle of the lesson through quick retrieval and use.
strategies.teacher_move: Ask students to retrieve three words from last lesson, match each to a meaning, then use one in today's sentence.
strategies.student_move: Students recall, match, and apply previously taught vocabulary.
coach_use_when: Vocabulary is taught once and not revisited.
Students forget terms between lessons.
Teacher needs a low-preparation routine for multiple exposures.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher notices which words are insecure and uses that information in the lesson.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives silent retrieval time before sharing answers.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher quickly identifies which vocabulary needs reteaching.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students retrieve and reuse words across spaced lessons.

implementation_steps: Choose three words from the previous lesson.
Ask students to recall or match meanings without notes first.
Have students use one word in today's context.
Reteach the word most students missed.
observable_indicators: Students retrieve before looking back.
Teacher scans responses.
One vocabulary gap informs immediate instruction.
cautions: Keep retrieval brief.
Do not turn vocabulary retrieval into a spelling-only activity.

strategies.title: Mini-Whiteboard Diagnostic Scan
strategies.summary: Teacher asks all students to show a response at once, scans patterns, and adjusts instruction immediately.
strategies.teacher_move: Ask one diagnostic question, say 'boards up in 3, 2, 1', scan for patterns, then decide whether to move on, reteach, or pair-correct.
strategies.student_move: Every student commits to an answer that the teacher can quickly inspect.
coach_use_when: Teacher relies on volunteers to judge understanding.
Misunderstandings are discovered only during marking.
The class moves on before most students are ready.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher uses whole-class response data to choose the next teaching move.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives all students time to think before boards up.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks every student's understanding, not only confident volunteers.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students who show partial understanding receive immediate re-exposure.

implementation_steps: Prepare one question that reveals the key misconception.
Have every student write a response.
Scan responses by pattern, not individual blame.
Choose one immediate response: move on, reteach, or peer compare.
observable_indicators: All students respond.
Teacher scans before revealing the answer.
Teacher's next step changes based on the scan.
cautions: Do not ask questions that only check copying.
Avoid naming students publicly for incorrect answers.

strategies.title: Stop-Check-Reteach Decision Point
strategies.summary: Teacher builds a planned pause into the lesson to diagnose understanding and choose a response.
strategies.teacher_move: After a short input or textbook example, stop and ask: 'Show me which step is unclear: A, B, or C'; reteach the most common barrier.
strategies.student_move: Students identify where understanding breaks down rather than silently continuing.
coach_use_when: Teacher completes textbook sections without checking learning during the lesson.
Students appear compliant but later produce inaccurate work.
EAL barriers emerge at a specific instruction step.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher uses student responses to reteach a precise barrier.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher pauses the task early enough for students to think and respond.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher identifies the step where understanding breaks down.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students receive a targeted second exposure to the unclear step.

implementation_steps: Identify a natural stopping point before independent work.
Ask students to indicate which step or word is unclear.
Count the most common barrier.
Reteach that barrier with a different example or representation.
observable_indicators: The lesson pauses before many students practise incorrectly.
Students identify a barrier.
Teacher reteaches one precise point.
cautions: Plan the stop point before the lesson.
Do not ask 'Does everyone understand?' as the diagnostic check.

strategies.title: Mini-Plenary Error Sort
strategies.summary: Teacher uses two or three anonymised errors mid-lesson so students diagnose misconceptions and improve work.
strategies.teacher_move: Display two anonymous responses and ask, 'Which one is stronger, what is the misconception, and how would we improve it?'
strategies.student_move: Students discuss errors as learning evidence and propose improvements.
coach_use_when: Teacher gives feedback only after collecting books.
Students repeat the same misconception during practice.
Class discussion needs a purposeful reason to talk.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher takes up student analysis of errors and links it to success criteria.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives pairs time to inspect the errors before whole-class discussion.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks whether students can recognise and correct a misconception.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students re-encounter the concept through error analysis and improvement.

implementation_steps: Collect two common anonymous errors during practice.
Pause the class for a three-minute mini-plenary.
Ask pairs to identify the misconception and improvement.
Students return to their own work and revise one answer.
observable_indicators: Errors are anonymous.
Students explain the misconception.
Students revise their own work after the discussion.
cautions: Use errors as class learning evidence, not as public criticism.
Select errors that reveal teachable misconceptions.

strategies.title: Exit Ticket to Group the Next Step
strategies.summary: Teacher uses a one-question exit ticket to decide the next lesson's grouping or reteach focus.
strategies.teacher_move: End with one question that reveals the lesson's key understanding; sort responses into 'secure', 'partial', and 'needs reteach' for next lesson planning.
strategies.student_move: Students independently show what they understand at the end of the lesson.
coach_use_when: Teacher is unsure what students learned from the lesson.
Next lessons are planned from textbook sequence only.
Feedback does not affect grouping or reteaching.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher uses exit ticket evidence to plan the next teaching move.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives independent thinking time before collecting responses.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher gathers individual evidence from every student.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students who are partial receive targeted re-exposure next lesson.

implementation_steps: Write one question linked to the lesson goal.
Students answer independently in the final three minutes.
Sort responses into three groups after the lesson.
Plan the first five minutes of the next lesson from the pattern.
observable_indicators: The exit question is diagnostic, not generic.
Teacher sorts responses by misconception or confidence.
Next lesson starts with evidence-based grouping or reteach.
cautions: Do not collect exit tickets without using them.
Keep the question short enough for independent completion.

strategies.title: Textbook Vocabulary Barrier Scan
strategies.summary: Teacher scans a textbook task for vocabulary barriers before teaching and plans quick language support.
strategies.teacher_move: Before students begin, ask: 'Which words on this page could stop students understanding the task?' then teach or annotate the top three.
strategies.student_move: Students use annotated or rehearsed vocabulary to access the textbook task.
coach_use_when: Teacher follows the textbook page without adapting for EAL learners.
Students misunderstand instructions or key terms.
Vocabulary demands are hidden inside word problems or explanations.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher checks whether students can use the words to explain the task.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives time to process vocabulary before task completion.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher verifies that students understand task language before independent work.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students see, hear, say, and apply the key task words.

implementation_steps: Read the textbook task from the perspective of an EAL learner.
Circle three words or phrases that could block understanding.
Teach or annotate those words before students start.
Ask one student to explain the task using the target words.
observable_indicators: Teacher adapts language access before the task.
Students can explain what the task is asking.
Vocabulary support is connected to the textbook activity.
cautions: Do not simplify the learning goal away.
Support access to the task rather than replacing thinking with copying.

strategies.title: Textbook Question Upgrade
strategies.summary: Teacher converts one closed textbook question into a dialogic question that requires explanation, evidence, or comparison.
strategies.teacher_move: Change 'What is the answer?' into 'How do you know?', 'Which method is better here?', or 'What evidence supports that answer?'
strategies.student_move: Students explain reasoning instead of only giving the textbook answer.
coach_use_when: Textbook work produces short answers and little talk.
Teacher asks mainly recall or answer-checking questions.
Students need practice explaining in English.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher probes reasoning and invites another student to build or compare.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher gives students time to form an explanation before answering.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher checks reasoning, not just answer accuracy.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students revisit the textbook item through explanation and peer comparison.

implementation_steps: Choose one textbook question that matters most.
Add one explanation stem: 'I know because ___.'
Ask pairs to explain before whole-class sharing.
Invite a second student to build, question, or compare methods.
observable_indicators: At least one textbook item becomes an explanation question.
Students use evidence or reasoning language.
Teacher follows up rather than only confirming the answer.
cautions: Upgrade one question first; do not redesign the whole textbook page at once.
Keep the question tied to the lesson goal.

strategies.title: Textbook Page to Learning Sequence
strategies.summary: Teacher turns a textbook page into a short learning sequence: prepare language, model one example, diagnose, then practise.
strategies.teacher_move: Plan the page as: '3 words, 1 model, 1 check, then practice' instead of starting with independent textbook work.
strategies.student_move: Students access vocabulary, see a model, show understanding, then practise with less confusion.
coach_use_when: Teacher begins lessons by assigning textbook exercises.
Students copy examples without understanding.
Lesson design lacks an early diagnostic point.

look_fors.response_uptake: Teacher checks student understanding before extended textbook practice.
look_fors.wait_time: Teacher includes processing time after the model and before practice.

jim_knight_big_four.checks_for_understanding: Teacher uses one diagnostic check between modelling and practice.

hits_multiple_exposures.exposure_pattern: Students encounter the concept through vocabulary, model, diagnostic check, and practice.

implementation_steps: Select the page section that matches the lesson goal.
Teach three barrier words.
Model one example while naming the thinking.
Use one diagnostic check before students practise independently.
observable_indicators: The textbook is used as a resource, not the full lesson plan.
Vocabulary is prepared before practice.
A check for understanding occurs before independent work.
cautions: Do not abandon the textbook; adapt it deliberately.
Keep the sequence simple enough for routine use.
